utterances: And why did you create this National African American Gun Association rather than just join the NRA?
I wanted a voice for our community where we could feel comfortable to gather, a place where we could come and learn about firearms together in a very peaceful and nonthreatening environment.
You're a gun owner.
Yes, I am.
May I ask what kind of guns you own?
I have a AK-47.
I have a Glock 19.
I have a Glock 17.
And I have the - as of late, I've got a hunting 783 Remington.
Why an AK-47?
It's a good gun.
It serves a purpose.
It's really good for self-defense purposes.
And I think it's a very solid tool to have.
What's your analysis, Mr. Smith - maybe from conversations with some of your membership - as to why you had this terrific increase in membership right after President Trump's inauguration?
Well, I think it's a three-headed monster.
I think, one, people are looking at, you know, the state of the United States in terms of robberies, burglaries, things that are happening on your daily basis.
Two, I think also - probably just as important - we see what's happening around the world.
You know, terrorism is something that scares black folks just like anybody else.
So we are aware of that.
But also, we look at what's happening in the political arena in terms of the conversations and the types of conversations.
We see folks that were on the fringes of society, let's say, eight, nine, 10 years ago.
But they're now out front talking about various things that are somewhat disturbing.
When you say people on the fringes of society, let me chance to be blunt.
Do you mean white racists?
I think there are some individuals out there that have negative views of African-Americans.
They have gotten bolder and bolder in their conversations in terms of our place in society.
We're getting too progressive.
Or we're moving too much up the ladder in society, so they want to kind of keep us down.
When we hear those things - and me being a black man - your alarms go off.
And you say, you know what?
I think the days of us sitting back and just turning the other cheek - I think those days are long gone.
Mr.
Smith, how do you and the African American Gun Association feel about stricter gun laws?
From the organizational standpoint, we believe that if you're a violent person and you beat your wife, you never need to own a gun.
If you have a violent felony, you never need to have a gun.
And a third piece - if you on - are the no-fly list, you need to not have a gun for any shape, form or reason.
And what about the argument - I'm sure you've heard - that putting more guns in the hands of more people just adds fuel to a fire that's burning up parts of America?
Well, see.
I disagree with that argument.
And I do hear that particular perspective, and I respect it.
I believe that the best way to stop someone with a gun other than law enforcement is to have a gun yourself.
I think too often as African-Americans we've been told to not arm ourselves.
And I think too often we are just sitting back and letting someone come and save us.
If you look at our communities right now across the country, they are in a wreck because we cannot protect those communities.
These communities are just literally wide-open markets for violence, and that needs to change.
What do you say, Mr. Smith, to those people who might point out that, actually, urban crime has been going down in the United States for a generation?
And I'm Mary Louise Kelly with more questions about how Facebook treats our personal data.
It is All Tech Considered.
(SOUNDBITE OF ULRICH SCHNAUSS' ""NOTHING HAPPENS IN JUNE"")
Because online video is getting so big.
YouTube and others, that's where younger viewers go.
What about the guy at the end of yesterday's report when you asked him about the best idea in TV?
That is an amazing question.
It could be worth many, many millions of dollars at the end of the day.
What a question.
He is TV.
He's a producer.
He makes shows.
So, I thought, here is a chance for some Hollywood glam, and we do lunch at the legendary Chateau Marmont.
There's a garden terrace like a Paris afternoon, as if everyone there were auditioning for a reality show called ""Swank."" A stunning blond two tables away, so still, her face never twitches.
I turn back to Jason.
Do you know what is going to happen to the networks in five years
I don't know.
I don't think anyone knows what's going to happen to the networks.
Jason Goldberg, a partner in Katalyst Films.
I believe that they will all live and I believe that things might just get a little bit leaner.
And I do feel, at the end of the day, that content will always be king.
Content is the term for programming.
Maybe it's shallow to use the words genius and television in the same sentence.
Okay, Jason is very adept at content for the audience that TV most wants.
Here we come.
(Soundbite of music)
Eighteen to 24-year-olds, not yet fully brand-loyal, but avid consumers of jeans, shampoos, soda, cars, and each other's culture.
(Soundbite of show, ""Punk'd"")
Being Punk'd is when you just get dissed.
Anything that makes you feel like an idiot, a moron.
With his partner, actor Ashton Kutcher, Jason created ""Punk'd"" on the cable channel that's for 18 to 24s, MTV.
We felt that there was nobody communicating to youth culture at all - honestly, communicating to youth culture.
Prank shows go way back in TV to ""Candid Camera."" ""Punk'd"" brought a generational freshness about style and attitude.
Nothing is staged.
It is spontaneity and its originality.
It's an energy.
(Soundbite of TV show, ""Punk'd"")
And here it is, and here we go, watch out because you're going to get ""Punk'd.
Punk'd"" was such a hit, MTV run the half hour episodes 50 times a week.
And Jason and Ashton hit again on MTV, same age-range, with ""Beauty and the Geek.
(Soundbite of TV show, ""Beauty and the Geek"")
A huge fan of ""Star-Trek""?
(Unintelligible) the trekkie thing.
Actually, the preferred term is trekker other than trekkie.
Now, they're looking for new projects and inspiration.
Something that I've been focused on for the last couple of years is what's happening overseas first then coming back in here.
See on old TV or online TV, you don't find 18 to 24s just by instinct.
Jason, who's 36, really does need the best idea in television.
Katalyst Film deploys street teams, hipster lookouts, who watch kids and what they do.
Now, this is not a new idea, but Jason and Katalyst executed extremely well, looking in places you might not expect.
He mentioned Oklahoma, industrial cities in the Midwest, where kids are so alienated and overlooked that they're inventing their own culture.
They're so true to themselves and so in their own world, you can't get to these kids.
They don't want to talk to you.
